perturbation.original_text: This Agreement shall be in full force and effect commencing on January 2, 2020 and shall remain in effect for one (1) year or until Consultant completes the services requested. Either Party shall have the right to terminate this Agreement without notice in the event of the bankruptcy, insolvency, or assignment for the benefit of creditors of the other Party. Either Party shall have the right to terminate this Agreement with notice, and the effective date of termination shall be the date such notice is received (by mail, overnight delivery, or fax) by the terminated Party.
perturbation.changed_text: This Agreement shall be in full force and effect commencing on January 2, 2020 and shall remain in effect for one (1) year or until Consultant completes the services requested. Either Party shall have the right to terminate this Agreement without notice in the event of the bankruptcy, insolvency, or assignment for the benefit of creditors of the other Party. The Company shall have the right to terminate this Agreement with notice, and the effective date of termination shall be thirty (30) days from the date such notice is received (by mail, overnight delivery, or fax) by the terminated Party. However, the Consultant shall only have the right to terminate this agreement after the initial one (1) year period, providing a sixty (60) day notice.
perturbation.explanation: This change introduces an in-text contradiction by creating asymmetrical termination rights. The Company can terminate with a 30-day notice, while the Consultant can only terminate after one year and must provide a 60-day notice. This contradicts the original clause which stated "Either Party shall have the right to terminate this Agreement with notice", creating uncertainty about whether both parties truly have equal termination rights and under what conditions, leading to disputes. This perturbation directly impacts the understanding and enforcement of termination conditions within the contract.
perturbation.contradicted_text: This Agreement shall be in full force and effect commencing on January 2, 2020 and shall remain in effect for one (1) year or until Consultant completes the services requested. Either Party shall have the right to terminate this Agreement without notice in the event of the bankruptcy, insolvency, or assignment for the benefit of creditors of the other Party. The Company shall have the right to terminate this Agreement with notice, and the effective date of termination shall be thirty (30) days from the date such notice is received (by mail, overnight delivery, or fax) by the terminated Party. However, the Consultant shall only have the right to terminate this agreement after the initial one (1) year period, providing a sixty (60) day notice.
perturbation.justification: The changed text introduces asymmetrical termination rights, granting the Company the right to terminate with a 30-day notice, while the Consultant can only terminate after one year and must provide a 60-day notice. This directly contradicts the earlier statement that "Either Party shall have the right to terminate this Agreement with notice," as it establishes different notice periods and eligibility, leading to a strong in-text contradiction and affecting the enforceability of the termination conditions.

perturbation.original_text: Consultant is not responsible for the performance of any services that may be rendered hereunder if the Company fails to provide the requested information in writing prior thereto. The services of Consultant shall not include the rendering of any legal opinions or the performance of any work that is in the ordinary purview of a certified public accountant. Consultant cannot guarantee results on behalf of the Company but shall use commercially reasonable efforts in providing the services listed above. Consultant's duty is to identify prospective acquisition/joint venture companies for the Company. Consultant will in no way act as a "broker-dealer" under state securities laws. Because all final decisions pertaining to any particular investment are to be made by the Company, the Company may be required to communicate directly with potential acquisition/joint venture prospective companies.
perturbation.changed_text: Consultant is not responsible for the performance of any services that may be rendered hereunder if the Company fails to provide the requested information promptly. The services of Consultant shall not include the rendering of any legal opinions or the performance of any work that is in the ordinary purview of a certified public accountant. Consultant cannot guarantee results on behalf of the Company but shall use commercially reasonable efforts in providing the services listed above. Consultant's duty is to identify prospective acquisition/joint venture companies for the Company. Consultant will in no way act as a "broker-dealer" under state securities laws. Because all final decisions pertaining to any particular investment are to be made by the Company, the Company may be required to communicate directly with potential acquisition/joint venture prospective companies.
perturbation.explanation: The change from "in writing prior thereto" to "promptly" creates an in-text contradiction regarding the required form and timing of information provision from the Company to the Consultant. Previously, it was explicitly "in writing", which is now removed, and the timing has changed from "prior thereto" which is now "promptly", which opens the door to interpretation. This ambiguity affects Section 9(e), where the Consultant relies on information provided by the company and can now argue that even if the information was not in writing, it was still 'promptly' provided, affecting the Consultant's level of diligence and the scope of the company's liability.
perturbation.contradicted_text: The Company covenants, represents and warrants to Consultant as follows: (a) Authorization. The Company and its signatories herein have full power and authority to enter into this Agreement and to carry out the transactions contemplated hereby. (b) No Violation. Neither the execution and delivery of this Agreement nor the consummation of the transactions contemplated hereby will violate any provision of the charter or by-laws of the Company or violate any terms of provision of any other material agreement to which the Company is a party or any applicable statute or law. (c) Contracts in Full Force and Effect. All contracts, agreements, plans, policies and licenses to which the Company is a party are valid and in full force and effect. (d) Consents. No consent of any person, other than the signatories hereto, is necessary to the consummation of the transactions contemplated hereby, including, without limitation, consents from parties to loans, contracts, lease or other agreements and consents from governmental agencies, whether federal, state, or local. (e) Consultant Reliance. Consultant has and will rely upon the documents, instruments and written information furnished to Consultant by the Company's officers or designated employees. (f) Company's Material. All representations and statements provided herein about the Company are true and complete and accurate. The Company agrees to indemnify, hold harmless, and defend Consultant, its officers, directors, agents and employees, at the Company's expense for any proceeding or suit which may rise out of any inaccuracy or incompleteness of any such material or written information supplied to Consultant.
perturbation.justification: The original text explicitly required information to be provided 'in writing prior thereto'. This requirement provided clarity and a paper trail, supporting the Company's warranty in Section 9(e) that 'Consultant has and will rely upon the documents, instruments and written information furnished to Consultant by the Company's officers or designated employees.' By changing 'in writing prior thereto' to 'promptly,' the revised text removes the requirement for written documentation and introduces ambiguity about the timing of information delivery. This weakens the Company's protection in Section 9(e) because the Consultant can now argue reliance on non-written or untimely information, potentially undermining the indemnity provided to the Consultant. This is a strong contradiction, leading to uncertainty regarding acceptable forms of communication.

perturbation.original_text: Any notices to be given hereunder by any Party to the other may be effected either by personal delivery in writing, by a reputable, national overnight delivery service, by facsimile transmission or by mail, registered or certified, postage prepaid with return receipt requested. Notices shall be addressed to the "Contact Person" at the addresses appearing on the signature page of this Agreement, but any Party may change his address or "Contact Person" by written notice in accordance with this subsection. Notices delivered personally shall be deemed delivered as of actual receipt, notices sent by facsimile shall be deemed delivered one (1) day after electronic confirmation of receipt, notices sent by overnight delivery service shall be deemed delivered one (1) day after delivery to the service, mailed notices shall be deemed delivered as of five (5) days after mailing.
perturbation.changed_text: Any notices to be given hereunder by any Party to the other may be effected either by personal delivery, by a reputable, national overnight delivery service, by facsimile transmission or by mail, registered or certified, postage prepaid with return receipt requested. Notices shall be addressed to the "Contact Person" at the addresses appearing on the signature page of this Agreement, but any Party may change his address or "Contact Person" by written notice in accordance with this subsection. Notices delivered personally shall be deemed delivered as of actual receipt, notices sent by facsimile shall be deemed delivered one (1) day after electronic confirmation of receipt, notices sent by overnight delivery service shall be deemed delivered one (1) day after delivery to the service, mailed notices shall be deemed delivered as of five (5) days after mailing.
perturbation.explanation: By removing "in writing" from the methods by which to serve a notice, now "personal delivery" can be made orally, which directly contradict's the requirement from parties to 'change his address or Contact Person by written notice' which is stated later in the paragraph. This creates uncertainty on whether or not you need to receive or send a notice in writing, and which notices apply with/without that method, affecting overall communication within the agreement and creating an in-text contradiction.
perturbation.contradicted_text: Any notices to be given hereunder by any Party to the other may be effected either by personal delivery, by a reputable, national overnight delivery service, by facsimile transmission or by mail, registered or certified, postage prepaid with return receipt requested. Notices shall be addressed to the "Contact Person" at the addresses appearing on the signature page of this Agreement, but any Party may change his address or "Contact Person" by written notice in accordance with this subsection. Notices delivered personally shall be deemed delivered as of actual receipt, notices sent by facsimile shall be deemed delivered one (1) day after electronic confirmation of receipt, notices sent by overnight delivery service shall be deemed delivered one (1) day after delivery to the service, mailed notices shall be deemed delivered as of five (5) days after mailing.
perturbation.justification: The removal of "in writing" from the notice provision allows for oral personal delivery of notices. This directly contradicts the requirement within the same section that any change to the "Contact Person" or address must be done by "written notice." The contradicted text is in the same paragraph as the changed text which demonstrates that there is a strong contradiction within the contract itself. This creates a clear inconsistency regarding the acceptable methods of notice and could lead to disputes over whether a notice was properly given, as the changed text permits oral notice for some actions but requires written notice for others.
